content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 19,
1994
Dennis Morita
Horton,
Knox, Carter
& Foote
Suite 101,
Law Building
895 Broadway
El Centro,
CA
92243
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—94—237
Dear Mr. Morita:
This is in response to your request for assistance under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.’!
Since you are not seeking advice concerning a specific
governmental decision and have not identified the public official
on whose behalf you are seeking advice,
your letter is considered
a request for informal assistance.
2
!
QUESTION
Who would be the “source of income” to a councilperson who is
employed by a mortgage lender and receives a commission on loans
he procures?
CONCLUS ION
The “source” of commission income would be the mortgage
lender for whom the councilperson is employed,
the borrower in the
transaction,
as well as any .developer who has a contract to refer
borrowers to the lender,
or receives a finder’s fee for referring
borrowers.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Page 2
Advice File No.
94—237
FACTS
You are requesting advice on behalf of a public official who
is employed by a mortgage lender.
The official receives income on
a commission basis when he arranges mortgage financing for a
borrower.
The commission in the transaction is paid for by the
borrower.
You indicated in your letter that a local developer may
refer home buyers to the mortgage lender, but that the buyer is
not obligated to use an particular lender.
The official was
previously employed as a real estate agent and is aware of
Commission regulations regarding sources of commission income.
There may be instances in the future where a developer who has
made referrals to the mortgage lender may be before the public
official’s agency on a particular issue.
He is concerned as to
who would constitute the “source of income” in these transactions.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial
interest in a governmental decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the
official’s immediate family,
or on:
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 8-7103(c)
Commission Regulation 18704.3 determines who is the source of
income in commission transactions.
For instance, the source of
commission income to a real estate agent would be:
(A)
The broker and brokerage business entity under
whose auspices the agent works;
(B)
The person the agent represents in the
transaction; and


Page
3
Advice File No.
94-237
(C)
Any person who receives a finder’s or other
referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to
the broker,
or who makes a referral pursuant to a
contract with the broker.
Conunission 18704.3(c) (3)
The regulation addresses typical occupations which derive
conuuission income.
Of course the list is not inclusive of all
possible occupations.
You stated in your letter that the official is employed by a
mortgage lender on a commission basis.
The arrangement to receive
a commission on loans appears to be part of the employment
relationship between the public official and his employer.
It is
thus appropriate to attribute commission income to parties in a
transaction, just as we attribute commission income in a real
estate transaction.
Thus, the employer is a source of income for
the full amount of the commission.
In your letter you indicated that the commission is paid by
the borrower.
Under Regulation 18702.4,
in a real estate
transaction the commission income is also attributable to the
party the agent represents.
The full commission income in the
mortgage lending transaction is attributable to the borrower, as
the person the official represents.
Finally,
as with real estate transactions, if a third party
receives a referral or finder’s fee on a mortgage transaction,
or
has a contract to refer borrowers to the mortgage lender,
commission income would be attributable to that person as well.
In your letter you indicated that developers may have
“arrangements” with the lender to refer prospective buyers for
possible financing.
The developer can refer a buyer to the
lender,
but the buyer is not under any obligation to go to any
specific lender.
In a subsequent letter,
you clarified this by
stating there is no written agreement between any developer and
the mortgage lender for such refrra1s and developers do not
receive a referral fee.
Accordingly,
we would attribute the commission income the
official receives for securing loans to his employer and to the
borrower in the transaction.
A developer would not be a source of
income unless the developer entered into a written agreement with


Page
4
Advice File No.
94—237
the lender or received a referral fee from the mortgage lender for
referring prospective buyers.
If you have any questions, or need to discuss this further,
please feel free to give me a call at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC/JET/jt

borrowers.;

Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
 •

Advice File No.
94—237
